decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)

requirements.text: Vendors are requested to submit proposals detailing target architecture and platform capabilities, high-level transition approach and indicative sequencing, operating model and support framework, and commercial and pricing structure.

Proposed solutions should support enterprise-scale deployments, secure connectivity, and integration with Kohler’s enterprise and manufacturing ecosystem, while enabling evaluation across multiple hosting options.

Proposals should support Kohler’s business objectives: transition from current on-premises Data Center toward modern hosting environments, reduce dependency on physical Data Center infrastructure, improve resilience, scalability and availability, enable standardized governance/security/compliance, support digital/analytics/AI use cases, and improve cost transparency and operating expenditure optimization.

The evaluation should ensure application performance and availability are maintained or improved, integration with manufacturing and enterprise systems is supported, and transition risks and business disruption are minimized.

Vendors are expected to propose a target operating model including roles and responsibilities/shared responsibility framework, governance/security/compliance approach, and operations and support model.
Detailed requirements, response structure, and evaluation inputs are provided in the Evaluation Template (Excel) shared as part of this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Critical response requirements are referenced in an external Excel file not included in the provided text.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is a common disqualifier hiding spot; the document requires an approach but does not specify a hard jurisdictional restriction in the provided text.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: No explicit permitted/prohibited data jurisdiction is stated; vendors must provide a region strategy and data residency approach.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios (Cloud, DCaaS, Hybrid) where applicable and clearly state assumptions.
Enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center are in scope.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Current environment is described as a Wisconsin-based on-premises Data Center.

requirements.text: In-scope components include COTS and internally developed applications; Windows and Linux virtual machines; databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL and other supported platforms; middleware and integration platforms; file storage and shared infrastructure services; backup and disaster recovery; IAM integrations; network connectivity across enterprise and manufacturing environments; monitoring, logging and security tooling; and selected OT-adjacent workloads limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.

A detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure was attached in the online RFP package; the inventory is comprehensive as of 29 May 2026 but subject to regular changes and provides counts and sizing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The baseline inventory is external to the provided text and is stated to drive sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning, and execution activities, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities such as code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.

Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope and should be factored in when the contract is in place.
Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The RFP distinguishes demonstrating capability/readiness from actually executing controls, certifications, or audits in this Phase 0 scope.

requirements.text: Tool implementation, automation deployment, integration development unless needed to illustrate solution capability, end-user training, change management execution, organizational transformation, data cleansing/transformation/archival, and detailed testing execution/validation including UAT, SIT, or performance testing are out of scope.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards. Evidence of certifications required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is buried in the compliance section and requires certification evidence, but the specific certifications are not named in the provided RFP body.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Conditional DCaaS-only compliance attestation requirement is in the compliance subsection and refers to another template for more information.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards are referenced but not specified in the provided text.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP Responses/Proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission timing and channel requirements can cause a proposal to be non-responsive before substantive evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is an easy-to-miss response-format gate: simple Yes/No answers are expressly insufficient.

requirements.text: All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided below.
All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory use of an external response template is easy to miss and may make a free-form proposal non-comparable or non-compliant.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP and non-SAP cost views are a specific pricing-format requirement that can be missed if pricing is submitted only as an aggregate total.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency compliance is a hard commercial formatting requirement buried in pricing guidelines.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Pricing currency must be United States Dollars.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
Payment terms are an important part of the proposal. Anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Net 90 appears in pricing/payment terms rather than eligibility; the language says anything less will not be competitive.

requirements.text: Vendors will be evaluated on target state architecture, security, and governance capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on migration approach, risk considerations, and transition strategy.
Vendors will be evaluated on networking and connectivity capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on availability, resilience, and disaster recovery approach.
Vendors will be evaluated on cost and commercial transparency.
Vendors will be evaluated on operating model and support capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on tools, ecosystem, platform maturity, and alignment to expected deliverables.

Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model, including indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework, and key roles and responsibilities.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality obligations are in Appendix 8.1 and can affect bid preparation behavior before submission.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party sharing restriction is in Appendix 8.1 and may affect subcontractors, partners, hyperscalers, or advisors used during bid preparation.

requirements.text: All responses submitted as part of this RFP will be treated as confidential and used strictly for evaluation purposes.

Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Appendix repeats solution compliance capability across industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations without defining the specific regimes.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are referenced but not enumerated.

requirements.text: Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw this RFP at any stage of the process, request additional information or clarification from vendors, and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler retains broad unilateral rights in the appendix; this is not a bidder eligibility requirement but is a contracting/procurement risk.

requirements.text: Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and service-level agreements will be defined and agreed upon during subsequent phases following vendor selection.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed legal and SLA obligations are deferred to later phases and are therefore not assessable from the provided RFP text.

similar_deals.title: Kohler Project Polaris — Global Manufacturing Hybrid Hosting / Data-Center Exit Phase-0 Advisory
similar_deals.outcome: Past proposal / WIP; no award outcome provided
similar_deals.why_relevant: It is the same client and substantially the same Project Polaris scope: Wisconsin Data Center, global manufacturing operations, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, target architecture, operating model, resilience, cost transparency, and transition strategy.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The strongest available pattern is a vendor-neutral 12-week Hosting Decision Lab with open-book USD TCO, landing-zone/security blueprints, transition sequencing, rollback gates, FinOps guardrails, and clear provider RACI; however, outcome is not evidenced as won.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant for global, multi-region manufacturing transformation with high availability and zero-downtime expectations, although it is SAP-specific rather than hosting-options advisory.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Manufacturing credibility, executive sponsor alignment, global follow-the-sun delivery, and zero-downtime methodology were decisive. For Kohler, bring senior sponsor alignment, manufacturing references, and a disruption-minimizing transition model.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to manufacturing IT operating-model transformation and enterprise service delivery, though less geographically aligned and not specifically cloud/DCaaS evaluation.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Fast mobilisation and sector credentials won the deal. For Kohler, a named team, two-week mobilisation plan, and early discovery/transition cell should be explicit.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant cautionary loss for a manufacturing transformation bid where compliance detail, certifications, and local/legal requirements were decisive.
similar_deals.key_lesson: A local entity requirement hidden in the compliance appendix was missed, causing the loss. For Kohler, every template, certification, data residency, contracting, payment, and local/regional requirement must be checked before submission.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation
similar_deals.why_relevant: Relevant to large-scale manufacturing IT transformation and risk-reduction positioning, although geography and likely scope differ from this advisory-focused RFP.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Proprietary IP and reusable accelerators reduced perceived delivery risk. For Kohler, use migration factories, landing-zone templates, observability patterns, FinOps assets, and operating-model playbooks.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 46%. The opportunity fit is strong because the past-proposal knowledge base contains a near-exact Kohler Project Polaris proposal with the right framing, scope, price point, 12-week fixed-price delivery model, vendor-neutral cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, landing-zone blueprint, open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, transition sequencing, rollback gates, and operating-model RACI. The deal corpus is also directionally supportive: DEAL-003, DEAL-089, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094 show that HCLTech can win manufacturing IT transformation work when it brings sector credibility, rapid mobilisation, global delivery, executive alignment, and risk-reducing IP. However, the probability is held below 50% because there is no public procurement award data to validate competitor pricing or incumbent advantage, the closest Kohler proposal has no evidenced win outcome, and the comparable wins are not exact hosting-options advisory/DCaaS evaluation awards. The RFP also contains multiple eliminatory risks around mandatory template use, detailed answers, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, submission channel/timeline, and net 90 payment terms. Procurement data does not show a competitor consistently winning this work, so no further competitor-driven reduction is applied, but the absence of procurement evidence limits confidence. Confidence level: medium-low, based mainly on the past proposal and deal corpus, with no public procurement validation.
capability_gaps: Compliance evidence gap: the RFP requires evidence of applicable industry/regional hosting certifications; the available proposal pattern references security/governance and certifications generally, but the bid must attach current ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, cloud partner, data center/DCaaS, and regional compliance evidence rather than describe capability only.

DCaaS attestation gap if bidding a DCaaS option: the RFP explicitly requires DCaaS providers to provide compliance attestations; the past proposal covers DCaaS as an evaluated option but the available intelligence does not confirm named DCaaS provider attestations are packaged.

Mandatory response-template execution gap: the RFP states simple Yes/No answers are insufficient and use of the Vendor Response template is mandatory; the proposal approach is strong, but the team must ensure every Excel requirement is answered with detailed narrative, assumptions, evidence, diagrams, and references.

Region/data residency substantiation gap: the RFP requires region strategy and data residency approach; the past proposal mentions global operations and USD TCO, but the final response must provide region-by-region hosting, DR, support, and data handling assumptions for Kohler’s global footprint.

Commercial compliance gap: the RFP requires USD pricing and says payment terms below net 90 are not competitive; the past proposal is USD-priced, but final commercial approval must explicitly accept net 90 or the bid will be commercially disadvantaged.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led, vendor-neutral hosting decision: position Project Polaris as a manufacturing resilience, cost, architecture, and operating-model decision rather than a generic cloud migration; use the prior Kohler proposal pattern of comparing public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration options through open-book USD TCO.

Zero-disruption manufacturing transition: emphasize application performance, plant/enterprise availability, dependency mapping, transition sequencing, rollback gates, hypercare, and a fee-at-risk or warranty mechanism tied to maintaining or improving availability during evaluation and transition planning.

Manufacturing-grade hybrid operating model: provide a clear shared-responsibility RACI across Kohler, incumbents, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, and HCLTech; include L1/L2/L3 support, global follow-the-sun operations, observability, SRE/AIOps, incident/problem management, governance, security, and compliance forums.

Landing-zone and governance blueprint for scalable digital/AI growth: show multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone principles, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency design, DR/HA patterns, FinOps guardrails, and security-by-design controls.

Rapid mobilisation with proven manufacturing credentials: draw from DEAL-089 and DEAL-003 lessons by committing to fast mobilisation, named leaders, manufacturing references, global delivery, and reusable accelerators/templates that reduce delivery risk.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so there is no ground-truth benchmark for incumbent advantage, competitor pricing, or Kohler’s historical buying pattern; this materially limits confidence and prevents an evidence-based competitor displacement assessment.

The best past-proposal match is for the same Kohler Project Polaris opportunity but appears to be a WIP/submitted proposal record with no explicit win outcome; it proves fit and preparation, not buyer preference or award likelihood.

Potential incumbent/provider risk: the prior proposal mentions a Cognizant/vendor coexistence model, implying existing providers may have embedded knowledge or relationships; without evidence of HCLTech executive alignment at Kohler, relationship risk remains material.

Eliminatory compliance risk is high because the RFP has strict submission channel/timeline, mandatory template, detailed-answer requirement, certification evidence, USD pricing, and net 90 commercial terms.

Comparable deal corpus is supportive but not exact: wins such as DEAL-003 and DEAL-089 are manufacturing transformations, not specifically hosting-options advisory/DCaaS evaluation for Kohler; therefore they improve credibility but should not be treated as direct proof of win likelihood.

Commercial scope ambiguity: the RFP has no stated estimated size or duration, while the past proposal assumes a 12-week fixed-price advisory at approximately $279,533; misalignment between buyer expectations and proposed scope/price could reduce competitiveness.

DCaaS scenario risk: if HCLTech proposes DCaaS through partners, third-party compliance attestations, SLAs, hosting locations, data residency, and contractual accountability must be explicit or the response may score poorly.
must_fix_before_bidding: Complete an appendix-by-appendix compliance review before submission, specifically validating certifications, data residency, local contracting/entity requirements, DCaaS attestations, and all Vendor Response template fields to avoid a DEAL-056-style compliance loss.

Attach current certification evidence and provider attestations, not just claims: ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II where applicable, cloud competency certifications, data center/DCaaS compliance attestations, and security/control mappings.

Convert the strong 12-week Hosting Decision Lab narrative into the mandatory Kohler Vendor Response template with detailed answers for Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid scenarios, avoiding any simple Yes/No responses.
Confirm commercial acceptability of USD-only pricing and net 90 payment terms; keep the prior $279,533 fixed-price advisory benchmark only if scope remains a 12-week evaluation/decision package.

Name the mobilisation team, Wisconsin Data Center transition cell, executive sponsor, architects, security lead, FinOps lead, and operating-model lead; historical wins show mobilisation speed and named accountability materially improve win chances.

ghost_bids.recommended_counter_strategy: Win with a vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower: complete the mandatory Vendor Response Template in detail; provide Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid reference architectures; include multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone design; show Prod/Test/Dev segregation; identity-provider integration; hybrid connectivity; region and data-residency strategy; DR/HA/scalability patterns; manufacturing and enterprise-system integration; compliance certification evidence and DCaaS attestations if applicable; per-application workload-placement scoring; transition waves; RACI/shared-responsibility model; FinOps/TCO model in USD; transparent unit-rate card; pass-through cloud/DCaaS economics; and net-90 payment terms. Commercially, because no total deal size is provided, avoid unsupported total-price claims and instead price in USD with auditable assumptions and a floor-aware structure competitive against Capgemini/Accenture while challenging Infosys/TCS sustainability where their margin evidence implies higher delivery-cost multipliers.

critical_success_factors: Compliance-perfect submission: mandatory template, detailed answers, deadline adherence, USD pricing, and net 90 terms.
Vendor-neutral workload placement methodology across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, edge/plant, and SaaS integration.
Evidence-led baseline using Kohler's inventory, dependency mapping, current cost, performance, service levels, security controls, contracts, and operational constraints.
Credible multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone architecture with identity integration, environment segregation, network segmentation, observability, backup, DR, and security controls.
Manufacturing-aware hybrid connectivity and resilience design, including plant/edge continuity and controlled OT-adjacent integration patterns.
Concrete operating model: shared responsibility framework, RACI across Kohler, incumbent vendors, hyperscalers, DCaaS providers, and support teams.
Open-book TCO and FinOps model with transparent assumptions, licensing treatment, OPEX optimization, migration/run cost separation, and scenario comparison.
Demonstrable compliance posture with certification evidence, regional/data residency strategy, and DCaaS attestations where applicable.
Transition sequencing with rollback gates and minimal business disruption, while respecting that execution migration and refactoring are future phases.
Strong executive-level decision package: options, trade-offs, risk register, commercial comparison, governance model, and recommended roadmap Kohler can defend internally.
Local Wisconsin Data Center engagement plus global delivery capability; this combination matters more than low-cost delivery alone.
References and accelerators in manufacturing, SAP/Oracle/SQL workloads, enterprise hosting transformation, cloud landing zones, and data center exit planning.
common_failure_modes: Using simple Yes/No responses or a lightly populated template, which the RFP explicitly says is insufficient.
Missing the mandatory Vendor Response template, Excel evaluation inputs, deadline, USD pricing, or net 90 commercial gate.
Treating the opportunity as a generic cloud migration rather than a hosting-options evaluation across cloud, DCaaS, hybrid, retained data center, plant/edge, and SaaS integration.
Over-promising migration execution, application refactoring, remediation, or re-platforming even though these are out of scope for this phase.
Failing to provide certification evidence and DCaaS compliance attestations; generic security language will be weak and may be non-compliant.
Underestimating manufacturing constraints: plant connectivity, latency, OT-adjacent read-only integrations, local continuity, SAP/Oracle/SQL/SAP HANA dependencies, and business-disruption risk.
Not producing credible open-book TCO/FinOps analysis including licensing, run cost, migration cost, resiliency tiers, network cost, and operating-model implications.
Losing to the incumbent because the bid lacks current-state insight, dependency mapping, or a believable coexistence RACI.
Appearing biased toward one hyperscaler or one DCaaS model before doing workload-by-workload placement analysis.
Providing high-level architecture without concrete landing-zone, identity, environment segregation, DR, monitoring, backup, and security operating-model detail.
Ignoring future legal/SLA uncertainty and accepting broad obligations without pricing assumptions and exclusions.
Failing to show local executive governance and Wisconsin Data Center presence, making the proposal look risky for a global manufacturer with live operations.
deal_fingerprint.recommended_bid_no_bid_decision: BID WITH CONDITIONS. This is a strong thematic fit and the knowledge base contains a proven Kohler-specific 12-week Hosting Decision Lab pattern, but the deal has real compliance traps, unknown Excel scoring gates, unknown deal size, net 90 commercial pressure, and likely incumbent/top-tier competition. No-bid if we cannot access the inventory/templates, accept net 90, provide certification evidence, involve needed partners under confidentiality rules, or secure stakeholder workshops for a credible TCO and operating-model design.
bid_conditions: Bid only if we can obtain and fully complete the mandatory Vendor Response template and Evaluation Template; a narrative-only or shallow template response is a disqualification risk.
Submit by Kohler's 26 June deadline through the designated channel with a named compliance owner and final red-team review.
Quote all pricing in USD and accept net 90 payment terms; anything less is explicitly non-competitive.
Provide specific compliance evidence, not generic claims: ISO 27001/SOC 1/SOC 2 or equivalent cloud/DCaaS attestations, plus data residency and regional control mapping.
If proposing DCaaS or partner-hosted options, secure explicit attestations from the DCaaS provider and confirm confidentiality permissions before involving hyperscalers/subcontractors.
Position the offer as a vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab across cloud, DCaaS, retained DC, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration, not as a pre-decided hyperscaler migration.
Require access to the application/infrastructure inventory and stakeholder workshops; no-bid if Kohler expects a credible TCO and target architecture without data access.

Include architecture diagrams, multi-account/multi-subscription landing-zone design, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, DR/HA patterns, and workload placement criteria.
Name a Wisconsin Data Center transition/governance cell and manufacturing-continuity approach; this deal will not be won by a generic offshore-only cloud factory response.
Use a sharp fixed-price advisory package around the proven 12-week decision-lab pattern; avoid over-scoping full migration execution because migration and refactoring are expressly out of scope.

draft.rfp_id: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary: Kohler’s Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise; it is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose enterprise platforms, Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality, analytics, connected products, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the target state is evaluated. Eric Tabor, Kohler’s Chief Digital Officer, has publicly linked the extended Cognizant partnership to operational and business excellence, productivity, responsiveness to business needs, and more customer value. HCLTech’s proposal translates that direction into an evidence-led, vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower that complements the incumbent ecosystem rather than assuming a clean-slate replacement. Over 36 months, beginning with a 12-week Polaris Decision Lab, we will build workload-by-workload placement scorecards across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS options; define an Azure-first landing-zone and governance blueprint; produce an open-book USD TCO and FinOps model; and sequence priority migration waves with rollback gates and manufacturing hypercare. Our recommended co-managed hybrid modernization is priced at $30,667,392, with net-90 commercial terms and fee-at-risk governance tied to protecting application performance and availability.
win_themes: Landing-zone and governance blueprint for scalable digital/AI growth
Rapid mobilisation with proven manufacturing credentials
Vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower binding Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid into one auditable decision model

sections.content: Project Polaris sits at the intersection of manufacturing uptime, enterprise modernization, data growth, and provider accountability. Kohler is evaluating hosting options while Cognizant publicly supports enterprise systems, cloud platforms, data-center operations, cybersecurity, observability, and end-user services; therefore the practical question is not who can run infrastructure in isolation, but how Kohler can make auditable placement decisions across an active multi-provider environment. The RFP context, the absence of a stated data-center exit, and the ERP modernization posting referencing SAP ECC and harmonization of 25+ business processes point to a workload-by-workload decision model: what moves to cloud, what belongs in DCaaS, what remains retained, what must stay near plants, and what should shift toward SaaS. Manufacturing and supply-chain workloads require latency, integration, recovery, and cutover analysis that generic cloud assessments miss. Connected products, digital engagement, data, analytics, AI, and cybersecurity responsibilities require privacy-by-design, zero-trust controls, identity integration, and evidence packs that can stand up to governance scrutiny. Kohler also needs cost transparency beyond rate cards: USD run cost, migration cost, licensing implications, resilience tiers, and service-accountability KPIs. We will treat these as business-risk decisions, preserving manufacturing continuity while giving Kohler a defensible hosting strategy for scalable digital and AI growth.

HCLTech proposes a 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization anchored by the Hosting Option Control Tower. The Control Tower is vendor-neutral by design: every in-scope application receives a placement score across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration using common criteria for performance, resilience, security, data residency, SAP dependency, integration complexity, cost, migration risk, and operating accountability. Phase 0 is the retained 12-week Polaris Decision Lab, aligned to the historical $279,533 Kohler pricing pattern, which produces the baseline, decision model, landing-zone blueprint, TCO, and first-wave roadmap before larger execution commitments are made. The target blueprint is Azure-first without being Azure-only: multi-subscription principles, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, policy-as-code, secure hybrid connectivity, region and data-residency design, DR/HA patterns, FinOps guardrails, and attestation evidence packs. The solution coexists with Cognizant and other incumbent providers through a shared RACI, KPI governance, service-integration forums, and clear transition boundaries. Execution funds priority migration waves, SAP-aware dependency analysis, pilot workloads, testing, rollback gates, and manufacturing cutover planning. The 36-month recommended price is $30,667,392, comprising people, infrastructure, migration, governance, contingency, and margin. This creates the differentiated outcome Kohler needs: one auditable model binding hosting choices, landing-zone readiness, migration sequencing, and run-cost transparency.

Our methodology is evidence-led, low-risk, and decision-oriented. In Mobilise, we establish the Wisconsin and global governance cadence, confirm scope, agree data requests, activate the Control Tower repository, and name workstream leads for architecture, SAP, network, security, FinOps, SRE, migration, and service integration. In Baseline, we inventory applications, infrastructure, interfaces, service levels, costs, contracts, tools, controls, and incidents; map dependencies; and separate enterprise, manufacturing, hospitality, digital, analytics, SAP, database, edge, and SaaS patterns. In Evaluate, we apply workload scorecards and compare cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS options with open-book USD TCO, resilience tiers, licensing impacts, and operating responsibilities. In Blueprint, we define landing zones, identity, connectivity, monitoring, security controls, data residency, DR/HA, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, and FinOps/TBM governance. In Roadmap, we design migration waves, pilots, testing, rollback gates, hypercare, and retained-scope decisions, prioritising zero unplanned manufacturing disruption attributable to HCLTech activities. During Execution, approximately 30 average FTEs deliver platform engineering, migration, SRE/AIOps, security, network, DBA, FinOps, ITSM, and service delivery with follow-the-sun support. Governance includes weekly delivery forums, monthly KPI reviews, quarterly executive steering, risk and dependency boards, and fee-at-risk warranty mechanisms tied to maintaining or improving availability during evaluation and transition planning. This approach makes Polaris executable without forcing a high-risk big-bang exit.

HCLTech will mobilise a manufacturing-continuity-first team that combines onsite Wisconsin leadership with global follow-the-sun depth. The core team includes an Engagement Partner, Polaris Control Tower Lead, Enterprise Architect, SAP Hosting Architect, Cloud Landing-Zone Lead, DCaaS and Data Center Architect, Network and Edge Architect, Security and Compliance Lead, SRE/AIOps Lead, FinOps/TBM Lead, Migration Factory Lead, Service Integration Lead, and PMO. They are supported by approximately 30 average FTEs across platform engineering, migration, database, network, security, observability, ITSM, and service delivery. Our credentials align to the risk profile of Project Polaris: global IT services delivery, manufacturing infrastructure experience, hybrid cloud and data-center operations, SAP-aware dependency mapping, landing-zone engineering, FinOps, ServiceNow-enabled governance, cybersecurity, and AIOps/observability. We will bring reusable accelerators rather than generic slides: application inventory templates, placement scorecards, migration-wave planning patterns, RACI models, cost-model structures, policy-as-code baselines, resilience-tier definitions, and compliance evidence-pack templates. The team’s mandate is not to displace the incumbent ecosystem; it is to give Kohler governance clarity, vendor accountability, and execution capacity while protecting plants and business platforms. Rapid mobilisation and proven hybrid-delivery discipline reduce delivery risk from day one.

Kohler should choose HCLTech because our offer is structured around the decision Kohler must make, not the platform a provider wants to sell. The Hosting Option Control Tower gives executives an auditable, comparable, USD-based view of public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS choices, including placement scores, run costs, migration costs, resilience trade-offs, security evidence, and operating accountability. Our approach protects manufacturing availability through dependency mapping, pilot waves, rollback gates, hypercare, SRE/AIOps observability, and fee-at-risk governance. It supports scalable digital and AI growth through Azure-first landing-zone principles, identity integration, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, secure connectivity, policy-as-code, data residency, FinOps guardrails, and readiness for Microsoft Fabric, Azure OpenAI, Databricks, ServiceNow, GenAI, and Agentic AI patterns where Kohler elects to use them. It also respects the incumbent strategic partnership by defining coexistence, RACI, KPI governance, and transition boundaries. HCLTech brings the pace, neutrality, manufacturing awareness, and commercial transparency Project Polaris requires. Together, we can convert hosting uncertainty into a controlled roadmap for resilience, cost discipline, innovation, and customer value.

critical_issues: Recommended price of $30,667,392 is not supported and is contradicted by the most relevant Kohler Project Polaris knowledge-base proposal, which lists a 12-week fixed-price engagement at $279,533.42.
Infosys margin claim of Q4 FY2026 operating margin = 20.9% conflicts with the supplied SEC/EDGAR financial data, which lists Infosys operating margin as 37.22%.

Most competitor claims rely on external sources not present in the raw data, including careers postings, press releases, LCA notices, client wins, headcount, operating margins, and revenue figures for TCS, Capgemini, and Accenture.
Accenture financial calculations are unsupported because the supplied SEC/EDGAR data explicitly says Accenture financial data is unavailable.
The agent cites comparable deal-corpus wins DEAL-003, DEAL-089, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094, but no such deal-corpus records are included in the raw data sources.

Several asserted RFP compliance risks are unsupported by the supplied raw data, including mandatory response template, detailed-answer requirement, net 90 payment terms, DCaaS attestations, and specific certification requirements.

The statement that the public procurement source states 'no public procurement data available' is inaccurate as written; procurement data is supplied, although it contains no relevant Kohler or competitor hosting-options award evidence.
removed_claims: Pricing confidence of 0.46, if tied to the unsupported $30.7M price.
References to DEAL-003, DEAL-089, DEAL-038, and DEAL-094 as comparable HCLTech wins.
Infosys current Wisconsin hiring in Milwaukee tied to its Manufacturing unit.
Infosys GlobalFoundries press release June 23, 2026 and any conclusions based on it.
Infosys Q4 FY2026 operating margin = 20.9% and the derived 1.264x delivery-cost floor.
TCS FY26 operating margin = 25.0% and the derived 1.333x delivery-cost floor.
TCS Elopak and ABB press-release win claims.
Capgemini 2026 operating-margin guidance = 13.6%–13.8% and the derived pricing floors.
Capgemini Aptiv press release, SAP/Siemens release references, and careers-presence claims.
Accenture Q3 FY2026 revenue, operating margin, adjusted FY2026 margin outlook, headcount, and revenue-per-employee calculation.
Accenture Milwaukee, Greenfield, Pleasant Prairie, Molson Coors, Uline, LCA, Dragos, runZero, NetRise, Databricks, OT/xOT cybersecurity, and Industry X activity claims.
Any competitor likelihood rating of 'high' that depends on unsupported hiring or press-release evidence.
RFP mandatory template requirement and claim that simple Yes/No answers are insufficient.
RFP net 90 payment-terms requirement and commercial disadvantage claim.
RFP-specific certification evidence requirements for ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, cloud partner certifications, data center/DCaaS, and regional compliance, unless separately evidenced.
Claims that public procurement data was unavailable; should be restated as no relevant Kohler/hosting-options competitor award data was found in the supplied procurement records.
warnings: The win-probability rationale has a solid basis in the near-exact Kohler Project Polaris proposal, but the absence of a win/loss outcome, unsupported comparable deal references, and unsupported RFP-risk details require reducing the claimed 0.46 probability.
The strongest verified pricing anchor is $279,533.42 for the 12-week Kohler Hosting Decision Lab; no raw source supports a multimillion-dollar or $30M+ bid.

Competitor analysis should be rewritten using only supplied data: procurement records do not show relevant Kohler awards, and SEC/EDGAR only provides usable competitor financial data for Infosys among the named competitors.

The killer differentiator is partially supported by the knowledge-base proposal's vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, workload-placement, landing-zone, TCO, FinOps, transition sequencing, rollback gates, and RACI content, but competitor-specific contrasts based on external press/careers evidence should be removed or caveated as unsupported.
Cloud pricing data is mentioned only as 'Validated via Azure/AWS APIs' with no actual figures, assumptions, workloads, or calculations; it cannot validate the recommended total price.
